1: The revelation of God: God's observation of evil widespread among human beings — the opening of the chapter — assumes the divine moral clarity that sees what is wrong in creation. The NT presents Christ as the one through whom this divine judgment becomes redemptive rather than merely condemning (John 3:17).
2: The theme: God gives wealth, riches, and honor — yet withholds the capacity to enjoy it, so a stranger devours it. This disconnection between blessing and enjoyment names a specifically theological problem: the good of creation turned to bitter vapor by the absence of the ability to receive it. Christ, the one who is himself the fullness of God's blessing (Ephesians 1:3), gives his people not only good things but the Spirit who enables them to receive creation's goods with thanksgiving (1 Timothy 4:4).
3: The theme: the man who cannot fill himself with good things and receives no burial — worse than a stillborn — represents the extreme case of a life without real participation in created goodness. Against this, Christ is the "resurrection and the life" who promises not existence without fullness but abundant life (John 10:10). He is also the one who received proper burial (Matthew 27:57–60), and who promises his people a resurrection body.
4: The theme: the stillborn comes in vapor and departs in darkness, its name buried in darkness — the limit of a life that never registered. Christ, whose own name is above every name (Philippians 2:9), names and knows each of his own; no life in him departs unnamed into darkness (John 10:3).
5: The revelation of God: the stillborn finds more rest than the one who lived long without good. The theme of rest — what constitutes true rest — runs through Wisdom literature into Hebrews, where the "rest that remains for the people of God" is entered through Christ, who himself rested on the seventh day (Hebrews 4:9–10). The rest the stillborn finds by not enduring a vapor-life is the shadow of the eternal rest Christ secures through his finished work.
6: The theme: "does not everyone go to the same place in the end?" — the leveling force of death that all human diversity cannot escape. Christ transforms this universal limit: his resurrection means that for his people, "the same place" at the end is not merely death but life out of death (Romans 6:8).
7: The theme: all labor toward filling the mouth, yet the appetite is never satisfied — the structural insatiability of the creature that no earthly labor can resolve. Christ as the Bread of Life (John 6:35) answers this: "Whoever comes to me will never be hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty." The satiation Qohelet cannot find under the sun is given in the one who comes from above.
8: The theme: what advantage does the wise person have over the fool in any final sense? Wisdom gives navigational skills but not ultimate escape from the common fate. The NT's answer is that wisdom finds its ultimate advantage in Christ: "God has made him to be for us wisdom" (1 Corinthians 1:30), and this wisdom leads through death to resurrection, not merely through life more skillfully.
9: The theme: better what the eyes can actually see than the restless wandering of desire — a limited commendation of the present moment over restless projection. This theme of presence over projection finds its NT deepening in Christ's command not to worry about tomorrow (Matthew 6:34), and Paul's learning of contentment in whatever state he is in (Philippians 4:11) through Christ who strengthens.
10: The revelation of God: "no one can contend with one far more powerful" — the sovereignty of God over the creature stands firm. Whatever is named is already known and subject to the one who named it (Genesis 2:19). The NT presents Christ as the one through whom and for whom all things were created and in whom they hold together (Colossians 1:16–17) — the one who is "far more powerful" taking on flesh without surrendering his sovereignty.
11: The theme: the more words, the more futility — a principle that the prologue of John inverts. The eternal Word, through whom all things were made (John 1:3), enters the world not to multiply futility but to resolve it; he is the Word that does not return empty (Isaiah 55:11). The futility of human words is healed by the one Word that carries the weight of divine meaning.
12: The theme: "who knows what is truly good for a person" and "who can tell anyone what will happen after they are gone?" — the double unknowing of mortal existence. Christ answers both: he is the Way, the Truth, and the Life who defines what is truly good (John 14:6), and he is the first to return from the dead and report what lies beyond (1 Corinthians 15:20). The two questions Qohelet poses as unanswerable are answered in the resurrection.
